addedBlocks:
## vvx
Job Jockey Kit - MCP tools for project initiative management

attachment.content:
- rbk-prep-release: Prepare release candidate for upstream delivery
- vvc-BREAK-LOCK: Force-break a stuck VVC lock after a crash.
- vvc-commit: Guarded git commit
- cma-prep-pr: Prepare branch for upstream PR contribution
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

BURP profile authored at .buk/rbmu_users/bhyslop/bujn-winpc/burp.env with three required fields pointing at operator-generated ed25519 keypairs in ~/.ssh/id_ed25519_winpc-{admin,workload} (no passphrase per bus_keys_operator_owned + the ssh-keygen -y derivation requirement). Fenestrate executed cleanly: first run dropped to /dev/tty for the Windows admin password, installed admin pubkey to administrators_authorized_keys, locked ACLs via icacls, wrote PasswordAuthentication=no + PubkeyAuthentication=yes + PermitEmptyPasswords=no into sshd_config, validated via sshd -t, restarted the service, and phase-2 reconnected key-only with verification green. Idempotent re-run skipped the password path entirely and re-verified the hardened state under key auth alone. Diagnostic improvement validated in both states observed end-to-end: empty roster (pre-burp.env) showed the 'No profiles found' branch + author hint + rnl pointer; populated roster (post-burp.env) showed 'Available investitures: bujn-winpc' + rnl pointer. Verification: bash -n green on all three edited shells, BUK self-test 28/28, rbtd fast suite 98 cases green pre-fenestrate. burp.env included in this commit despite station-local /Users/bhyslop/... paths — operator chose to track for now; gitignore decision deferred.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                              
e81a5dc1  ₣A-       Strengthen paddock-posture rule to forbid coronets in paddock prose. The prior wording prohibited only retrospective coronet annotations ("BBAAM landed"-style); a real failure surfaced where the AAF/AAE/AAD coronets were embedded prospectively in the §Heat Sequence section and silently rotted when AAF was dropped. Replacement promotes the rule from "don't annotate" to absolute "coronets do not appear in paddock prose" — covers retrospective, prospective, and cross-reference shapes — and names the principle (coronet = enrollment-ledger key, not shape data) so future readers can extend the rule beyond the examples. Firemarks remain explicitly permitted (heats change rarely). JJS0 surveyed and intentionally not modified: the spec carries paddock structure/operations only, not content-posture; layering stays clean.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                         
2ca5d4dc  ₢A-AAu    Vocabulary unification follow-up to AAu: rename BURN identifier from 'viceroyalty' to 'investiture' so BURN and BURP use the same noun. After AAu collapsed BURP to singleton-per-(user, viceroyalty) with file-presence-enforced 1:1 correspondence between BURP investiture and BURN viceroyalty directory names, the divergent vocabulary (viceroyalty for BURN, investiture for BURP) became non-load-bearing — both quoins now name the same string. Two parallel quoins in distinct regime domains: {burn_investiture} (BURN-defined office) and {burp_investiture} (per-station-user grant into that office); the directory-name equality is unchanged, only the noun unifies. content.content:
1	# jjezs_paddock ₣A-
2	
3	## Goal
4	
5	Windows test infrastructure for Recipe Bottle. Run RB's container isolation
6	tests on a Windows host with WSL, Cygwin, and PowerShell access paths.
7	
8	## Current Concept
9	
10	**Jurisdiction** is the BUK feature-area for reaching out to remote nodes
11	deterministically. Logical-only umbrella concept; `bujb_jurisdiction.sh` is
12	its implementation seat (BCG-compliant module housing both verbs). Neither
13	the umbrella nor the implementation module earns AXLA-entity voicing in
14	BUS0. Architectural spine in BUS0 §Remote Node Access.
15	
16	**Two regimes (BURN, BURP):**
17	
18	- BURN — node-shape (host, OS family). Per-node, git-transported, changes
19	  rarely. Identifier: investiture.
20	- BURP — privileged + workload credentials. Per-station-user. Three fields:
21	  `BURP_PRIVILEGED_USER`, `BURP_PRIVILEGED_KEY_FILE`,
22	  `BURP_WORKLOAD_KEY_FILE`. Pubkeys derived from privkeys via
23	  `ssh-keygen -y` at use-time; no separate pubkey paths or inline strings.
24	  Identifier: investiture — by construction equal to a registered
25	  investiture. The BURP directory name must match a BURN profile dir;
26	  the 1:1 correspondence is enforced by file-presence check at load time.
27	
28	**BURC addition:**
29	
30	- `BURC_WORKLOAD_USER` — project-wide convention name for the workload OS
31	  user that garrison provisions on every node. Single source of truth.
32	
33	**Two verbs (Fenestrate, Garrison):**
34	
35	- **Fenestrate** — Windows-OpenSSH-only `sshd_config` harden plus admin SSH
36	  key-trust establishment. Uniform across shell-letters (no variant).
37	  Two-phase ceremony forced by `Restart-Service sshd` killing its own
38	  session. Tabtarget `buw-jpF` (capital F = persistent config application).
39	- **Garrison** — workload account provisioning only. Per-shell-letter
40	  (b/c/w). Never reads or writes any `sshd_config`; never alters admin
41	  authorized_keys. First action is admin SSH via key auth; failure surfaces
42	  immediately.
43	- For Linux/Mac there is no fenestrate verb — admin trust is operator-manual
44	  (e.g., `ssh-copy-id`); garrison runs against an existing key-trusted admin
45	  foothold. For Cygwin/WSL sshd hardening on Windows nodes (in addition to
46	  Windows OpenSSH), hardening is operator-manual.
47	
48	**Garrison-destructive model:**
49	
50	- Every garrison wipes prior workload state regardless of which investiture
51	  or operator runs it. Node runtime state belongs to whoever last garrisoned.
52	- One workload user per node, ever. No conscript / discharge / withdraw /
53	  vanquish / inventory verbs; garrison subsumes them.
54	- Admin-side sshd configuration is fenestrate's scope on Windows;
55	  operator-manual elsewhere.
56	
57	**Cross-cutting premises:**
58	
59	- `burn_host_singularity` — host of a node lives only in BURN; BURP reaches
60	  it by reference, never duplicates.
61	- `bus_keys_operator_owned` — system never generates or modifies SSH key
62	  material; operator owns all key administration.
63	
64	**Tabtarget colophons (14 total; b/c/w shell support, PowerShell deferred):**
65	
66	- `buw-r[np][lrv]` — BURN/BURP regime config (List/Render/Validate) — 6
67	- `buw-jpF` — Fenestrate (Windows OpenSSH only; uniform) — 1
68	- `buw-jpG[bcw]` — Garrison per shell-letter (b=native bash for Linux/Mac;
69	  c=Cygwin; w=WSL) — 3
70	- `buw-jwk` — Knock (probe workload reachability) — 1
71	- `buw-jwc` — Run command file as workload (shell determined by garrison) — 1
72	- `buw-jws` — Interactive SSH as workload — 1
73	- `buw-hj0` — Handbook jurisdiction top — 1
74	
75	**Operational seat (`bujb_jurisdiction.sh` + `bujb_cli.sh`):**
76	
77	BCG-compliant module housing both fenestrate and garrison implementations,
78	hardcoding the three shell-letter→`command=` directive mappings (b/c/w)
79	used by garrison on the workload account, the workload privkey destination
80	path on remote per shell-letter, the canonical WSL distribution name
81	(`rbtww-main`), and the Windows OpenSSH `sshd_config` hardening directive
82	set used by fenestrate. SSH transport mechanics live in code; BURP carries
83	no shell selection.
84	
85	**Fenestrate ceremony (Windows OpenSSH only; uniform):**
86	
87	Two-phase remote-run because `Restart-Service sshd` terminates its own
88	ssh session.
89	
90	Phase 1 (over the password-or-key admin SSH session):
91	
92	1. SSH-as-`BURP_PRIVILEGED_USER` with
93	   `PreferredAuthentications=publickey,password`. Steady-state:
94	   `BURP_PRIVILEGED_KEY_FILE` authenticates by key. First-run: ssh prompts
95	   on /dev/tty; operator types the Windows admin password once. Fenestrate
96	   process never sees the password.
97	2. Idempotently place admin pubkey (derived from `BURP_PRIVILEGED_KEY_FILE`
98	   via `ssh-keygen -y`) in `administrators_authorized_keys` as a bare entry
99	   (no `command=`; fenestrate is uniform). Performed before any sshd_config
100	   change so key auth is available for phase 2's reconnect.
101	3. `icacls` lockdown on `administrators_authorized_keys`.
102	4. Write hardened directives to `sshd_config`: `PubkeyAuthentication yes`,
103	   `PasswordAuthentication no`, `PermitEmptyPasswords no`. Idempotent.
104	5. Verify written state via PowerShell `Get-Content` returning raw bytes,
105	   then bash-side BCG-compliant matching extracting effective directive
106	   values. Keeps PowerShell out of parsing (its error semantics are
107	   unreliable).
108	6. `sshd -t` validates the config (penultimate atomic op of phase 1; a
109	   malformed config aborts fenestrate before it could brick running sshd).
110	7. `Restart-Service sshd` (final atomic op of phase 1; ssh session
111	   terminates here — bash orchestrator treats the disconnect as the
112	   expected phase boundary).
113	
114	Phase 2 (key auth only):
115	
116	8. Reconnect as `BURP_PRIVILEGED_USER` with
117	   `PreferredAuthentications=publickey` (no password fallback). Failure
118	   here surfaces a brick — the operator's manual password no longer opens
119	   a session.
120	9. Verify post-state: the admin session authenticates by key alone and the
121	   running sshd serves the hardened configuration.
122	
123	**Garrison ceremony (per shell-letter; workload-only):**
124	
125	1. SSH-as-`BURP_PRIVILEGED_USER` with `BURP_PRIVILEGED_KEY_FILE`; key auth
126	   only (no password fallback). On Windows, presupposes fenestrate has run
127	   for this investiture. On Linux/Mac, admin trust is operator-managed
128	   (e.g., `ssh-copy-id` placed the admin pubkey).
129	2. Destroy existing workload user (if present) via `userdel -r` or platform
130	   equivalent — removes account + home directory; stray files outside home
131	   are workload's own concern, not garrison's.
132	3. Create fresh workload user named `BURC_WORKLOAD_USER` (unprivileged: no
133	   sudo, no admin group, ssh-only access).
134	4. Place workload pubkey in workload's authorized_keys with the
135	   shell-letter's `command=` directive.
136	5. Copy workload privkey to remote workload account at the path hardcoded in
137	   `bujb_jurisdiction.sh` per shell-letter (so workload can authenticate
138	   outbound to GitHub).
139	6. Validate round-trip — SSH as workload + exec no-op + verify exit 0.
140	
141	## Heat Sequence
142	
143	**Spec foundation:**
144	- spec-bus0-jurisdiction — BUS0 rewrite reflecting current concept
145	- introduce-fenestrate-narrow-garrison — split admin trust + sshd harden
146	  out of garrison into a uniform fenestrate verb
147	
148	**Implementation:**
149	- implement-jurisdiction — four-field BURP, `BURC_WORKLOAD_USER`,
150	  `bujb_jurisdiction.sh` module, 14 tabtargets, fenestrate + garrison
151	  ceremonies, cleanup superseded colophons + handbook entries
152	
153	**Investigation (independent, runs anytime):**
154	- AAe — localhost-fundus-parallel-saturation diagnosis
155	
156	**Practice (Windows host, blocks on implementation):**
157	- AAD — WSL distro + Cygwin install
158	- AAE — JJK fundus inside WSL
159	- AAF — Docker dual-daemon
160	
161	## Deferred (named for future return)
162	
163	- **PowerShell garrison + dispatch** — `buw-jpGp` tabtarget + the `p`
164	  shell-letter mapping in `bujb_jurisdiction.sh`. Current heat ships b/c/w
165	  only.
166	- **Adopt** — cross-station-user pubkey install via existing privileged trust.
167	  Today: each station user runs the ceremony fresh from their own machine.
168	- **User regime** — centralized per-user pubkey for shared admin scenarios.
169	- **Separate workload→GitHub keypair** — current shape reuses workload SSH
170	  keypair for outbound; tighten to per-purpose separation if security demands.
171	- **BURP override fields for non-conventional key layouts** — operators with
172	  centralized SSH key storage, non-`.pub`-suffix layouts.
173	- **Localhost garrison/dispatch variants** — deferred; remote nodes only.
174	- **Git normalization on remote** — JJK has `jjx_plant`; BUK doesn't duplicate.
175	- **Cygwin / WSL sshd hardening verb** — fenestrate covers Windows OpenSSH
176	  only. If c/w garrisons ever require their own sshd to be hardened
177	  programmatically (rather than operator-managed), that is a future
178	  fenestrate sibling.
179	
180	## Standing Notes
181	
182	- **Fenestrate handles first-time admin trust establishment on Windows.**
183	  On a fresh node, ssh's `PreferredAuthentications=publickey,password` falls
184	  through to a /dev/tty password prompt; operator types the Windows admin
185	  password once. Fenestrate then installs the admin pubkey, sets the icacls
186	  ACL, hardens sshd_config (`PasswordAuthentication no`), restarts sshd,
187	  and reconnects to verify by key. Subsequent runs use key auth
188	  automatically. The operator's manual Windows scope reduces to: install
189	  OpenSSH server, enable the service, set `PasswordAuthentication yes`
190	  temporarily, allow the firewall port, ensure the admin user has a known
191	  password.
192	- **Linux/Mac admin trust is operator-managed.** No fenestrate verb;
193	  operator places admin pubkey via `ssh-copy-id` (or equivalent), and
194	  garrison's first admin SSH succeeds by key alone.
195	- **Two-phase fenestrate is structural, not optional.** `Restart-Service
196	  sshd` kills its own ssh session, so any post-restart verification must
197	  happen over a fresh session. Bash orchestrator treats the post-restart
198	  disconnect as the expected phase boundary, not a failure.
199	- **Encrypted (passphrase-protected) privkeys not supported.** `ssh-keygen -y`
200	  prompts interactively; fenestrate and garrison would hang. Operator
201	  policy: privkeys unencrypted; station-level security covers
202	  privkey-at-rest protection.
203	- **Workload pubkey must be pre-registered with GitHub out-of-band.** Operator
204	  pastes the pubkey into GitHub (deploy key per repo or personal SSH key)
205	  before garrison runs. Garrison cannot validate this; it is a precondition.
206	- **WSL distribution is hardcoded to `rbtww-main`** in
207	  `bujb_jurisdiction.sh`. If the current default WSL distribution differs,
208	  garrison-WSL fails fast with a clear error.
209	- **Some `BURP_PRIVILEGED_USER` values may contain spaces** (e.g., Windows
210	  admin user `b hyslop`); audit shell quoting at every variable expansion and
211	  path construction.
212	- Tailscale provides stable transport (Mac sees `rocket` as `100.x.y.z` /
213	  tailnet hostname); not adopted as a BUK dependency.
214	- PowerShell-from-WSL pattern: bash owns control flow, PowerShell is the
215	  Windows syscall layer; one atomic command per call.
216	- `command=` in authorized_keys is OpenSSH-standard (used by gitolite, GitHub,
217	  rsync, borg); shrinks key blast radius rather than expanding it.

file.content:
# jjezs_paddock ₣A-

## Goal

Windows test infrastructure for Recipe Bottle. Run RB's container isolation
tests on a Windows host with WSL, Cygwin, and PowerShell access paths.

## Current Concept

**Jurisdiction** is the BUK feature-area for reaching out to remote nodes
deterministically. Logical-only umbrella concept; `bujb_jurisdiction.sh` is
its implementation seat (BCG-compliant module housing both verbs). Neither
the umbrella nor the implementation module earns AXLA-entity voicing in
BUS0. Architectural spine in BUS0 §Remote Node Access.

**Two regimes (BURN, BURP):**

- BURN — node-shape (host, OS family). Per-node, git-transported, changes
  rarely. Identifier: investiture.
- BURP — privileged + workload credentials. Per-station-user. Three fields:
  `BURP_PRIVILEGED_USER`, `BURP_PRIVILEGED_KEY_FILE`,
  `BURP_WORKLOAD_KEY_FILE`. Pubkeys derived from privkeys via
  `ssh-keygen -y` at use-time; no separate pubkey paths or inline strings.
  Identifier: investiture — by construction equal to a registered
  investiture. The BURP directory name must match a BURN profile dir;
  the 1:1 correspondence is enforced by file-presence check at load time.

**BURC addition:**

- `BURC_WORKLOAD_USER` — project-wide convention name for the workload OS
  user that garrison provisions on every node. Single source of truth.

**Two verbs (Fenestrate, Garrison):**

- **Fenestrate** — Windows-OpenSSH-only `sshd_config` harden plus admin SSH
  key-trust establishment. Uniform across shell-letters (no variant).
  Two-phase ceremony forced by `Restart-Service sshd` killing its own
  session. Tabtarget `buw-jpF` (capital F = persistent config application).
- **Garrison** — workload account provisioning only. Per-shell-letter
  (b/c/w). Never reads or writes any `sshd_config`; never alters admin
  authorized_keys. First action is admin SSH via key auth; failure surfaces
  immediately.
- For Linux/Mac there is no fenestrate verb — admin trust is operator-manual
  (e.g., `ssh-copy-id`); garrison runs against an existing key-trusted admin
  foothold. For Cygwin/WSL sshd hardening on Windows nodes (in addition to
  Windows OpenSSH), hardening is operator-manual.

**Garrison-destructive model:**

- Every garrison wipes prior workload state regardless of which investiture
  or operator runs it. Node runtime state belongs to whoever last garrisoned.
- One workload user per node, ever. No conscript / discharge / withdraw /
  vanquish / inventory verbs; garrison subsumes them.
- Admin-side sshd configuration is fenestrate's scope on Windows;
  operator-manual elsewhere.

**Cross-cutting premises:**

- `burn_host_singularity` — host of a node lives only in BURN; BURP reaches
  it by reference, never duplicates.
- `bus_keys_operator_owned` — system never generates or modifies SSH key
  material; operator owns all key administration.

**Tabtarget colophons (14 total; b/c/w shell support, PowerShell deferred):**

- `buw-r[np][lrv]` — BURN/BURP regime config (List/Render/Validate) — 6
- `buw-jpF` — Fenestrate (Windows OpenSSH only; uniform) — 1
- `buw-jpG[bcw]` — Garrison per shell-letter (b=native bash for Linux/Mac;
  c=Cygwin; w=WSL) — 3
- `buw-jwk` — Knock (probe workload reachability) — 1
- `buw-jwc` — Run command file as workload (shell determined by garrison) — 1
- `buw-jws` — Interactive SSH as workload — 1
- `buw-hj0` — Handbook jurisdiction top — 1

**Operational seat (`bujb_jurisdiction.sh` + `bujb_cli.sh`):**

BCG-compliant module housing both fenestrate and garrison implementations,
hardcoding the three shell-letter→`command=` directive mappings (b/c/w)
used by garrison on the workload account, the workload privkey destination
path on remote per shell-letter, the canonical WSL distribution name
(`rbtww-main`), and the Windows OpenSSH `sshd_config` hardening directive
set used by fenestrate. SSH transport mechanics live in code; BURP carries
no shell selection.

**Fenestrate ceremony (Windows OpenSSH only; uniform):**

Two-phase remote-run because `Restart-Service sshd` terminates its own
ssh session.

Phase 1 (over the password-or-key admin SSH session):

1. SSH-as-`BURP_PRIVILEGED_USER` with
   `PreferredAuthentications=publickey,password`. Steady-state:
   `BURP_PRIVILEGED_KEY_FILE` authenticates by key. First-run: ssh prompts
   on /dev/tty; operator types the Windows admin password once. Fenestrate
   process never sees the password.
2. Idempotently place admin pubkey (derived from `BURP_PRIVILEGED_KEY_FILE`
   via `ssh-keygen -y`) in `administrators_authorized_keys` as a bare entry
   (no `command=`; fenestrate is uniform). Performed before any sshd_config
   change so key auth is available for phase 2's reconnect.
3. `icacls` lockdown on `administrators_authorized_keys`.
4. Write hardened directives to `sshd_config`: `PubkeyAuthentication yes`,
   `PasswordAuthentication no`, `PermitEmptyPasswords no`. Idempotent.
5. Verify written state via PowerShell `Get-Content` returning raw bytes,
   then bash-side BCG-compliant matching extracting effective directive
   values. Keeps PowerShell out of parsing (its error semantics are
   unreliable).
6. `sshd -t` validates the config (penultimate atomic op of phase 1; a
   malformed config aborts fenestrate before it could brick running sshd).
7. `Restart-Service sshd` (final atomic op of phase 1; ssh session
   terminates here — bash orchestrator treats the disconnect as the
   expected phase boundary).

Phase 2 (key auth only):

8. Reconnect as `BURP_PRIVILEGED_USER` with
   `PreferredAuthentications=publickey` (no password fallback). Failure
   here surfaces a brick — the operator's manual password no longer opens
   a session.
9. Verify post-state: the admin session authenticates by key alone and the
   running sshd serves the hardened configuration.

**Garrison ceremony (per shell-letter; workload-only):**

1. SSH-as-`BURP_PRIVILEGED_USER` with `BURP_PRIVILEGED_KEY_FILE`; key auth
   only (no password fallback). On Windows, presupposes fenestrate has run
   for this investiture. On Linux/Mac, admin trust is operator-managed
   (e.g., `ssh-copy-id` placed the admin pubkey).
2. Destroy existing workload user (if present) via `userdel -r` or platform
   equivalent — removes account + home directory; stray files outside home
   are workload's own concern, not garrison's.
3. Create fresh workload user named `BURC_WORKLOAD_USER` (unprivileged: no
   sudo, no admin group, ssh-only access).
4. Place workload pubkey in workload's authorized_keys with the
   shell-letter's `command=` directive.
5. Copy workload privkey to remote workload account at the path hardcoded in
   `bujb_jurisdiction.sh` per shell-letter (so workload can authenticate
   outbound to GitHub).
6. Validate round-trip — SSH as workload + exec no-op + verify exit 0.

## Heat Sequence

**Spec foundation:**
- spec-bus0-jurisdiction — BUS0 rewrite reflecting current concept
- introduce-fenestrate-narrow-garrison — split admin trust + sshd harden
  out of garrison into a uniform fenestrate verb

**Implementation:**
- implement-jurisdiction — four-field BURP, `BURC_WORKLOAD_USER`,
  `bujb_jurisdiction.sh` module, 14 tabtargets, fenestrate + garrison
  ceremonies, cleanup superseded colophons + handbook entries

**Investigation (independent, runs anytime):**
- AAe — localhost-fundus-parallel-saturation diagnosis

**Practice (Windows host, blocks on implementation):**
- AAD — WSL distro + Cygwin install
- AAE — JJK fundus inside WSL
- AAF — Docker dual-daemon

## Deferred (named for future return)

- **PowerShell garrison + dispatch** — `buw-jpGp` tabtarget + the `p`
  shell-letter mapping in `bujb_jurisdiction.sh`. Current heat ships b/c/w
  only.
- **Adopt** — cross-station-user pubkey install via existing privileged trust.
  Today: each station user runs the ceremony fresh from their own machine.
- **User regime** — centralized per-user pubkey for shared admin scenarios.
- **Separate workload→GitHub keypair** — current shape reuses workload SSH
  keypair for outbound; tighten to per-purpose separation if security demands.
- **BURP override fields for non-conventional key layouts** — operators with
  centralized SSH key storage, non-`.pub`-suffix layouts.
- **Localhost garrison/dispatch variants** — deferred; remote nodes only.
- **Git normalization on remote** — JJK has `jjx_plant`; BUK doesn't duplicate.
- **Cygwin / WSL sshd hardening verb** — fenestrate covers Windows OpenSSH
  only. If c/w garrisons ever require their own sshd to be hardened
  programmatically (rather than operator-managed), that is a future
  fenestrate sibling.

## Standing Notes

- **Fenestrate handles first-time admin trust establishment on Windows.**
  On a fresh node, ssh's `PreferredAuthentications=publickey,password` falls
  through to a /dev/tty password prompt; operator types the Windows admin
  password once. Fenestrate then installs the admin pubkey, sets the icacls
  ACL, hardens sshd_config (`PasswordAuthentication no`), restarts sshd,
  and reconnects to verify by key. Subsequent runs use key auth
  automatically. The operator's manual Windows scope reduces to: install
  OpenSSH server, enable the service, set `PasswordAuthentication yes`
  temporarily, allow the firewall port, ensure the admin user has a known
  password.
- **Linux/Mac admin trust is operator-managed.** No fenestrate verb;
  operator places admin pubkey via `ssh-copy-id` (or equivalent), and
  garrison's first admin SSH succeeds by key alone.
- **Two-phase fenestrate is structural, not optional.** `Restart-Service
  sshd` kills its own ssh session, so any post-restart verification must
  happen over a fresh session. Bash orchestrator treats the post-restart
  disconnect as the expected phase boundary, not a failure.
- **Encrypted (passphrase-protected) privkeys not supported.** `ssh-keygen -y`
  prompts interactively; fenestrate and garrison would hang. Operator
  policy: privkeys unencrypted; station-level security covers
  privkey-at-rest protection.
- **Workload pubkey must be pre-registered with GitHub out-of-band.** Operator
  pastes the pubkey into GitHub (deploy key per repo or personal SSH key)
  before garrison runs. Garrison cannot validate this; it is a precondition.
- **WSL distribution is hardcoded to `rbtww-main`** in
  `bujb_jurisdiction.sh`. If the current default WSL distribution differs,
  garrison-WSL fails fast with a clear error.
- **Some `BURP_PRIVILEGED_USER` values may contain spaces** (e.g., Windows
  admin user `b hyslop`); audit shell quoting at every variable expansion and
  path construction.
- Tailscale provides stable transport (Mac sees `rocket` as `100.x.y.z` /
  tailnet hostname); not adopted as a BUK dependency.
- PowerShell-from-WSL pattern: bash owns control flow, PowerShell is the
  Windows syscall layer; one atomic command per call.
- `command=` in authorized_keys is OpenSSH-standard (used by gitolite, GitHub,
  rsync, borg); shrinks key blast radius rather than expanding it.

# jjezs_pace ₢A-AAD

## Character
Human-driven with agent amending.

## Docket

Operator (Windows host): install WSL with distribution name
`rbtww-main` set as the default distribution. Per AAn's
ruthless-delete posture, detailed install instructions live in vendor
docs; `rbw-hw` carries only the downstream-load-bearing names. Cygwin
install is deferred to the follow-on Cygwin path pace, not part of
AAD.

Agent amends inlined `rbw-hw` operator prose if friction surfaces
(e.g., a missing constraint that breaks downstream AAr Garrison-WSL).

Goal: WSL distro `rbtww-main` functional and set as the default
distribution on the Windows host, ready for AAr jurisdiction
ceremonies (Fenestrate + Garrison-WSL).

content.text:
## Mounted ₣A- — rbk-mvp-3-draft-windows-procedures

**Racing heats**: ₣BB, ₣BA, ₣A_, ₣A-, ₣A7, ₣A6, ₣Ah, ₣AU

**Heat status**: 19 / 24 paces done. Remaining: AAD, AAE, AAr (Garrison-WSL), AAF (Docker), and a final practice pace.

**Paddock summary**: BUK Jurisdiction infrastructure for reaching Windows nodes. Two regimes (BURN node-shape, BURP credentials), two verbs (Fenestrate for Windows admin trust + sshd harden, Garrison for workload provisioning per shell-letter b/c/w). Currently in the **Practice** sequence on the Windows host, after the spec foundation and implementation paces landed.

**Recent work**: AAt practiced fenestrate end-to-end against `bujn-winpc` (key-only admin trust + sshd hardening verified on idempotent re-run); AAu collapsed BURP to singleton-per-(user, investiture) with file-presence enforcement.

---

### Next: ₢A-AAD — `practice-environment-procedures` [rough]

**Character**: Human-driven with agent amending.

**Goal**: Get WSL distro `rbtww-main` installed and set as the **default** distribution on the Windows host, ready for the downstream AAr Garrison-WSL pace. Cygwin install is explicitly deferred to a later pace.

**Agent role**: Stand by while operator runs the install on the Windows host. If friction surfaces — particularly anything that would break downstream Garrison-WSL — amend the inlined operator prose in `rbw-hw` (the Windows handbook tabtarget) so the constraint is captured. Per AAn's ruthless-delete posture, vendor install detail belongs in Microsoft's docs; only downstream-load-bearing names live in `rbw-hw`.

---

### Name assessment

The silks `practice-environment-procedures` is generic — the docket is narrowly scoped to "WSL `rbtww-main` install + set default." Worth a relabel? Something like `practice-wsl-rbtww-install`. Optional — the existing name is unambiguous in context since Cygwin is split out.

### Execution strategy

- **Model tier**: opus (current) is appropriate — friction-amendment requires judgment about what's load-bearing vs. ephemeral install detail.
- **Parallelization**: none — single-operator workflow on the Windows host.
- **Active mode**: passive amender. I'll wait for you to drive the WSL install and report any friction or surprises. When you hit something `rbw-hw` doesn't anticipate, I'll edit the inlined prose.

Ready to proceed — relabel first, or start the install?

#!/bin/bash
#
# Copyright 2026 Scale Invariant, Inc.
#

# WITHOUT WARRANTIES OR CONDITIONS OF ANY KIND, either express or implied.
# See the License for the specific language governing permissions and
# limitations under the License.
#
# Author: Brad Hyslop <bhyslop@scaleinvariant.org>
#
# Recipe Bottle Windows Handbook - rbhw_top function

set -euo pipefail

test -z "${ZRBHW0_SOURCED:-}" || return 0
ZRBHW0_SOURCED=1

rbhw_top() {
  zrbhw_sentinel

  buc_doc_brief "Display Windows handbook orchestrator — full setup sequence"
  buc_doc_shown || return 0

  buh_section  "Windows Test Infrastructure Setup"
  buh_line     "Complete setup sequence for running Recipe Bottle tests on a Windows host."
  buyy_cmd_yawp "${ZRBHW_WSL_DISTRO}"; local -r z_wsl_distro_yelp="${z_buym_yelp}"
  buh_line "   Target WSL distro: ${z_wsl_distro_yelp}"
  buyy_cmd_yawp "${ZRBHW_DOCKER_CONTEXT}"; local -r z_docker_context_yelp="${z_buym_yelp}"
  buh_line "   Docker context:    ${z_docker_context_yelp}"
  buh_e
  buh_section  "Phase 1: SSH Reachability (BUK — operator-manual prerequisite)"
  buh_tt       "  1. Jurisdiction handbook walkthrough:  " "${BUWZ_HJ0_TOP}"
  buh_line     "  2. SSH client key generation:          Generate SSH client key per vendor docs (e.g., 'ssh-keygen -t ed25519')"
  buh_e
  buh_section  "Phase 2: Admin Trust + sshd Harden (BUK — jurisdiction)"
  buh_tt       "  3. Fenestrate (admin SSH + harden):    " "${BUWZ_JP_FENESTRATE}" "" " <investiture>"
  buh_e
  buh_section  "Phase 3: Environments (BUK — generic OS install)"
  buh_line     "  4. WSL distribution setup:             Install WSL and create distribution per vendor docs"
  buh_line     "     Use distribution name: ${z_wsl_distro_yelp}"
  buh_line     "  5. Cygwin installation:                Install Cygwin POSIX userland per vendor docs"
  buh_e
  buh_section  "Phase 4: Workload Provisioning (BUK — jurisdiction)"
  buh_tt       "  6. Garrison-Cygwin (workload):         " "${BUWZ_JP_GARRISON_CYGWIN}" "" " <investiture>"
  buh_tt       "  7. Garrison-WSL (workload):            " "${BUWZ_JP_GARRISON_WSL}" "" " <investiture>"
  buh_e
  buh_section  "Phase 5: User Provisioning (JJK — fundus accounts)"
  buh_line     "  8. Fundus user provisioning runs inside WSL:"
  buh_tt       "     Phase 1 (create users):             " "${JJZ_FUNDUS_PHASE1}"
  buh_tt       "     Phase 2 (clone repos):              " "${JJZ_FUNDUS_PHASE2}"
  buh_e
  buh_section  "Phase 6: Docker (RBK — project-specific)"
  buh_tt       "  9. Docker Desktop install:             " "${RBZ_HW_DOCKER_DESKTOP}"
  buh_tt       " 10. Native dockerd in WSL:              " "${RBZ_HW_DOCKER_WSL_NATIVE}"
  buh_line "     Pass distro name: ${z_wsl_distro_yelp}"
  buh_tt       " 11. Docker context discipline:          " "${RBZ_HW_DOCKER_CONTEXT}"

}

# eof

#!/bin/bash
#
# Copyright 2026 Scale Invariant, Inc.
#
# Licensed under the Apache License, Version 2.0 (the "License");
# you may not use this file except in compliance with the License.
# You may obtain a copy of the License at

# See the License for the specific language governing permissions and
# limitations under the License.
#
# Author: Brad Hyslop <bhyslop@scaleinvariant.org>
#
# Recipe Bottle Windows Handbook - rbhw_docker_wsl_native function

set -euo pipefail

test -z "${ZRBHWDN_SOURCED:-}" || return 0
ZRBHWDN_SOURCED=1

rbhw_docker_wsl_native() {
  zrbhw_sentinel

  buc_doc_brief "Display native Docker daemon installation in WSL (arg: distro-name)"
  buc_doc_shown || return 0

  local z_distro="${BUZ_FOLIO:-}"
  test -n "${z_distro}" || buc_die "rbhw_docker_wsl_native: distro-name required"

  buh_section  "Native Docker Daemon in WSL"
  buyy_cmd_yawp "${z_distro}"; local -r z_distro_yelp="${z_buym_yelp}"
  buh_line "Install and run dockerd natively inside WSL distro ${z_distro_yelp}."
  buh_e
  buh_step1    "Enter the Distribution:"
  buh_code     "wsl -d ${z_distro}"
  buh_e
  buh_step1    "Install Docker Engine:"
  buh_code     "sudo apt update"
  buh_code     "sudo apt install -y docker.io"
  buh_code     "sudo systemctl enable --now docker"
  buh_e
  buh_step1    "Grant Docker Access to Users:"
  buh_line     "Add each user that needs Docker access to the docker group:"
  buh_code     "sudo usermod -aG docker USERNAME"
  buh_line     "Replace USERNAME with each fundus user account."
  buh_e
  buh_section  "Verification:"
  buh_code     "docker ps"
  buh_line     "Expect: empty container list (no errors)."

}

# eof

content.text:
Not switching — adding a second daemon. The plan is **dual-daemon** (per Phase 6 of `rbw-hw` and the AAF pace name in the heat sequence):

- **Docker Desktop** stays on Windows. Windows-side and **Cygwin** shells use it via the default context.
- **Native `dockerd`** runs inside the `rbtww-main` WSL distro (installed via `apt install docker.io` + systemd). WSL shells use it via a named docker context.
- `rbhw_docker_context_discipline` enforces deterministic selection: each shell environment has exactly one daemon it talks to.

So the import of AAD specifically:

1. **`rbtww-main` is the home for the native WSL daemon.** Whatever name you pick here gets baked into `bujb_jurisdiction.sh` (already hardcoded as `rbtww-main`) and into the `rbw-HWdw` Docker-WSL-native handbook (which takes the distro name as `param1`). If WSL's *default* distribution doesn't match, garrison-WSL fails fast.
2. **It's a fundus host first, Docker host second.** AAE provisions JJK fundus user accounts inside this distro (Phase 5); AAF then installs `dockerd` inside it (Phase 6) and adds those fundus users to the `docker` group. The same WSL distro carries both responsibilities.
3. **Three shells, three Docker realities.** Bash-on-Linux/Mac sees one daemon (its own); Cygwin and Windows-PowerShell talk to Docker Desktop; WSL talks to its native daemon. Test infrastructure that runs containers from inside WSL (the test path Recipe Bottle cares about) is using the native one — that's the load-bearing reason the WSL daemon exists at all. Docker Desktop's WSL2 backend is intentionally avoided here so RB tests aren't coupled to Desktop's lifecycle.

Does that match your model, or were you considering collapsing to one daemon?

lastPrompt: I need to better understand the import here.  We will be switching docker to run in this distro, correct?

message.content: Hold on, our current heat should not have anything to do with JJK fundus: we brok that concept with BURN/BURP and have _not_ prioritized reviving it.  Fundus as a concept should _only_ be JJK and deferred.  Yes, lets trust Docker Desktop default patterns for one docker daemon.  But I now have a question: docker likes to have a platform level 'socket' for 'docker in docker' stuff: are there implications about having a WSL universe docker as well as a windows default docker desktop docker running proximally?  They don't necessarily need to be at the same time but I definitely need to understand the real constraints here.  Websearch  if it helps.

Based on the search results, here's what I found about Docker Desktop WSL integration versus native dockerd inside WSL:

## The Core Conflict

Before installing Docker Desktop, it's recommended to uninstall any version of Docker Engine or the Docker CLI installed directly inside a WSL Linux distribution, as running both can cause conflicts.

## Why Conflicts Occur

All WSL2 distros run in the same network namespace, and users who tried to run Docker in multiple distros simultaneously experienced issues because the two Docker daemons created conflicting iptables rules.

## Architecture Differences

The search results reveal two distinct approaches:

1. **Docker Desktop**: The daemon runs inside a dedicated Docker Desktop WSL2 distribution (visible as docker-desktop), and the CLI tools are proxied into your development distributions.

2. **Standalone Docker Engine**: Standalone Docker Engine installs the Docker daemon directly inside your WSL2 distribution exactly as you would on a regular Linux server, with no GUI, no Windows integration, and no licensing restrictions, managed with systemctl.

## 2026 Recommendations

The recommendation is to evaluate Desktop versus engine-in-WSL based on policy, licensing, and automation needs, then standardize one path and enforce version checks in bootstrap.

Based on the search results, I found information about Docker Desktop's WSL integration settings. Here's what you should know:

## WSL Integration Configuration

When Docker Desktop starts, go to Settings > Resources > WSL Integration. The Docker-WSL integration is enabled on the default WSL distribution, which is Ubuntu.

You can control integration on a per-distribution basis. Interaction between Docker Desktop and other distributions only occurs when you explicitly enable WSL integration for those distributions. This means you can disable WSL integration for specific distributions while keeping it enabled for others on the same machine.

## Running Native dockerd in WSL Distros

While Docker Desktop supports running both Linux and Windows containers, you cannot run both simultaneously. To run simultaneous containers or if you prefer to install a container engine directly in your Linux distribution, you would need to install and run a separate Docker instance in WSL, or follow the Linux installation instructions for that container service.

This approach allows you to have Docker Desktop disabled for specific WSL distributions while running a native dockerd instance directly in those distributions on the same machine, giving you per-distro control over which Docker environment is used.

I've completed a web search on your query about WSL2, docker.sock, Docker Desktop integration, and iptables conflicts. Here are the key findings:

## Docker Socket Location and WSL2 Integration

Docker Desktop runs inside its own docker-desktop WSL distribution, isolated from other distributions in the same way any two WSL distributions are isolated from each other. Enabling WSL integration for a distribution gives you direct access to docker commands from that distribution's terminal — which is useful for Linux-native development workflows.

## iptables Conflicts in WSL2

One of the main issues is iptables compatibility. Docker uses iptables (a Linux firewall utility) to manage network rules for containers. WSL2's default iptables uses nftables (a newer framework), which Docker may not fully support.

In the original WSL2 architecture, running other applications that deal with network interfaces and iptables could create conflicts. All WSL2 distros run in the same network namespace, and there were reports of issues when trying to run docker in 2 distros at once, where the 2 Docker daemons killed each other by creating conflicting iptables rules.

## Solutions

For native Docker daemon in WSL2, some developers use a shared docker socket location at `/mnt/wsl/shared-docker/docker.sock`, and Debian requires additional configuration with `"iptables": false` in the docker daemon.json file.

Alternatively, switching to iptables-legacy instead of iptables-nft resolved the issue for some users.

Alternatively, switching to iptables-legacy instead of iptables-nft resolved the issue for some users.

lastPrompt: Hold on, our current heat should not have anything to do with JJK fundus: we brok that concept with BURN/BURP and have _not_ prioritized reviving it.  Fundus as a concept should _only_ be JJK and defe…

content.text:
Real constraints, in priority order:

**1. The hard hazard is shared kernel iptables, not the socket path.**

All WSL2 distros — including the hidden `docker-desktop` distro that hosts Docker Desktop's daemon — share one network namespace under a single Linux kernel. iptables/NAT rules live in that one kernel's netfilter tables. Two daemons running concurrently each call `iptables -t nat -A POSTROUTING ...` for their own bridge networks; they overwrite each other's chains and "kill each other," as the issue thread puts it. This is not a theoretical concern — it's the documented breakage mode for "dockerd in two distros at once."

**2. Socket-path collision is avoidable.**

Docker Desktop's WSL integration injects its socket proxy at `/var/run/docker.sock` *only in distros where you enable integration* (Settings → Resources → WSL Integration is per-distro). If you disable integration for `rbtww-main`, Desktop never touches that path inside it, and a native dockerd would own it cleanly. So the socket question has a clean toggle — the iptables question does not.

**3. "Not at the same time" is a real escape hatch, but operator-managed.**

If Docker Desktop is *stopped* (system-tray quit, not just minimized), its daemon and iptables rules are gone. Native dockerd inside `rbtww-main` then has the kernel to itself. Reverse direction: stop native dockerd's systemd unit, restart Desktop. This works; it's just lifecycle discipline rather than coexistence.

**4. DinD (mounting `/var/run/docker.sock` into a container) is orthogonal.**

That pattern just hands the daemon's control plane to the container. It works the same whichever single daemon is running. The dual-daemon question doesn't change DinD's risk profile.

---

### Implication for this heat

Given your "trust Docker Desktop default patterns for one docker daemon": collapse AAF from **dual-daemon** to **Docker-Desktop-only with WSL integration enabled for `rbtww-main`**. Concretely:

- AAD (current): just install WSL + set `rbtww-main` as default. **No Docker decision happens here** — your AAD scope stands.
- AAF (later): becomes "enable Docker Desktop's WSL integration for `rbtww-main`; verify `docker ps` from inside the distro reaches Desktop's daemon." Drop `rbhw_docker_wsl_native` (rbhwdn) from `rbw-hw`. The context-discipline procedure (rbhwcd) also collapses — there's only one context now.
- The `bujb_jurisdiction.sh` Docker-related hardcoding (if any beyond the WSL distro name) can shed assumptions about a separate native daemon.
- Native dockerd becomes a **Horizon** item — operator-managed exclusive-lifecycle if ever needed for test isolation.

### Implication for `rbw-hw` Phase 5 (JJK fundus)

Independent of the Docker decision: Phase 5 (`JJZ_FUNDUS_PHASE1`/`PHASE2`) is JJK fundus provisioning. Per your correction, fundus is JJK-only and currently deferred. That phase should leave `rbw-hw` for this heat. The downstream consequence: Phase 6 step "Add each fundus user to the docker group" needs a non-fundus name for whatever workload account *does* land — most likely `BURC_WORKLOAD_USER` from garrison.

### Where I'd take this

Two paddock-shape changes are now warranted, and these are larger than "amend `rbw-hw` operator prose":

1. Heat-sequence change: AAF docket character shifts from "dual-daemon" to "single-daemon Desktop integration." May warrant a reslate of AAF.
2. `rbw-hw` Phase 5 deletion + Phase 6 rewrite (drop rbhwdn invocation, drop rbhwcd invocation or rewrite, replace fundus-user docker-group reference).

Both are paddock/docket edits, not code edits. Want me to draft a paddock note + reslate AAF, or do you want to think about it before we touch state?

Sources:
- [Docker Desktop WSL 2 backend on Windows | Docker Docs](https://docs.docker.com/desktop/features/wsl/)
- [Develop with Docker Desktop using WSL 2 on Windows | Docker Docs](https://docs.docker.com/desktop/features/wsl/use-wsl/)
- [Docker on WSL2 without Docker Desktop — /dev/solita](https://dev.solita.fi/2021/12/21/docker-on-wsl2-without-docker-desktop.html)
- [Install Docker on Windows (WSL) without Docker Desktop — DEV Community](https://dev.to/bowmanjd/install-docker-on-windows-wsl-without-docker-desktop-34m9)
- [Running Docker on WSL2 without Docker Desktop (the right way) — DEV](https://dev.to/felipecrs/simply-run-docker-on-wsl2-3o8/comments)
- [WSL2 Kernel does not support `nftables` — microsoft/WSL #6655](https://github.com/microsoft/WSL/issues/6655)
- [Get started with Docker containers on WSL — Microsoft Learn](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/windows/wsl/tutorials/wsl-containers)

Any similar constraints for our macos support, which additionally is Docker Desktop, I believe?  Actually can you determine what macos install is, i.e. docker desktop for mac?

content.content:
## Goal

Windows test infrastructure for Recipe Bottle. Run RB's container isolation
tests on a Windows host with WSL, Cygwin, and PowerShell access paths.

## Current Concept

**Jurisdiction** is the BUK feature-area for reaching out to remote nodes
deterministically. Logical-only umbrella concept; `bujb_jurisdiction.sh` is
its implementation seat (BCG-compliant module housing both verbs). Neither
the umbrella nor the implementation module earns AXLA-entity voicing in
BUS0. Architectural spine in BUS0 §Remote Node Access.

**Two regimes (BURN, BURP):**

- BURN — node-shape (host, OS family). Per-node, git-transported, changes
  rarely. Identifier: investiture.
- BURP — privileged + workload credentials. Per-station-user. Three fields:
  `BURP_PRIVILEGED_USER`, `BURP_PRIVILEGED_KEY_FILE`,
  `BURP_WORKLOAD_KEY_FILE`. Pubkeys derived from privkeys via
  `ssh-keygen -y` at use-time; no separate pubkey paths or inline strings.
  Identifier: investiture — by construction equal to a registered
  investiture. The BURP directory name must match a BURN profile dir;
  the 1:1 correspondence is enforced by file-presence check at load time.

**BURC addition:**

- `BURC_WORKLOAD_USER` — project-wide convention name for the workload OS
  user that garrison provisions on every node. Single source of truth.

**Two verbs (Fenestrate, Garrison):**

- **Fenestrate** — Windows-OpenSSH-only `sshd_config` harden plus admin SSH
  key-trust establishment. Uniform across shell-letters (no variant).
  Two-phase ceremony forced by `Restart-Service sshd` killing its own
  session. Tabtarget `buw-jpF` (capital F = persistent config application).
- **Garrison** — workload account provisioning only. Per-shell-letter
  (b/c/w). Never reads or writes any `sshd_config`; never alters admin
  authorized_keys. First action is admin SSH via key auth; failure surfaces
  immediately.
- For Linux/Mac there is no fenestrate verb — admin trust is operator-manual
  (e.g., `ssh-copy-id`); garrison runs against an existing key-trusted admin
  foothold. For Cygwin/WSL sshd hardening on Windows nodes (in addition to
  Windows OpenSSH), hardening is operator-manual.

**Garrison-destructive model:**

- Every garrison wipes prior workload state regardless of which investiture
  or operator runs it. Node runtime state belongs to whoever last garrisoned.
- One workload user per node, ever. No conscript / discharge / withdraw /
  vanquish / inventory verbs; garrison subsumes them.
- Admin-side sshd configuration is fenestrate's scope on Windows;
  operator-manual elsewhere.

**Cross-cutting premises:**

- `burn_host_singularity` — host of a node lives only in BURN; BURP reaches
  it by reference, never duplicates.
- `bus_keys_operator_owned` — system never generates or modifies SSH key
  material; operator owns all key administration.

**Tabtarget colophons (14 total; b/c/w shell support, PowerShell deferred):**

- `buw-r[np][lrv]` — BURN/BURP regime config (List/Render/Validate) — 6
- `buw-jpF` — Fenestrate (Windows OpenSSH only; uniform) — 1
- `buw-jpG[bcw]` — Garrison per shell-letter (b=native bash for Linux/Mac;
  c=Cygwin; w=WSL) — 3
- `buw-jwk` — Knock (probe workload reachability) — 1
- `buw-jwc` — Run command file as workload (shell determined by garrison) — 1
- `buw-jws` — Interactive SSH as workload — 1
- `buw-hj0` — Handbook jurisdiction top — 1

**Operational seat (`bujb_jurisdiction.sh` + `bujb_cli.sh`):**

BCG-compliant module housing both fenestrate and garrison implementations,
hardcoding the three shell-letter→`command=` directive mappings (b/c/w)
used by garrison on the workload account, the workload privkey destination
path on remote per shell-letter, the canonical WSL distribution name
(`rbtww-main`), and the Windows OpenSSH `sshd_config` hardening directive
set used by fenestrate. SSH transport mechanics live in code; BURP carries
no shell selection.

**Fenestrate ceremony (Windows OpenSSH only; uniform):**

Two-phase remote-run because `Restart-Service sshd` terminates its own
ssh session.

Phase 1 (over the password-or-key admin SSH session):

1. SSH-as-`BURP_PRIVILEGED_USER` with
   `PreferredAuthentications=publickey,password`. Steady-state:
   `BURP_PRIVILEGED_KEY_FILE` authenticates by key. First-run: ssh prompts
   on /dev/tty; operator types the Windows admin password once. Fenestrate
   process never sees the password.
2. Idempotently place admin pubkey (derived from `BURP_PRIVILEGED_KEY_FILE`
   via `ssh-keygen -y`) in `administrators_authorized_keys` as a bare entry
   (no `command=`; fenestrate is uniform). Performed before any sshd_config
   change so key auth is available for phase 2's reconnect.
3. `icacls` lockdown on `administrators_authorized_keys`.
4. Write hardened directives to `sshd_config`: `PubkeyAuthentication yes`,

content.content: 118:Each [Vessel](#Vessel) is a directory under `rbev-vessels/` containing at minimum an `rbrv.env` configuration file; [Conjure](#Conjure) [Vessels](#Vessel) also include a Dockerfile. 168:- Multi-architecture support via `docker buildx` with binfmt emulation 216:To inspect an image's supply chain, [Plumb](#Plumb) its [provenance](#Provenance) — the full view shows the [SBOM](#SBOM), build info, and Dockerfile; the compact view summarizes the attestation chain. 248:[Establishing](#Establish) walks the [Payor](#Payor) through project creation, API enablement, and consent screen configuration — the manual prerequisites before any automated operations can run. 291:**[Reliquary](#Reliquary)** — Co-versioned set of builder tool images (skopeo, docker, gcloud, syft) inscribed from upstream into the [Depot's](#Depot) registry. 310:[Grafting](#Graft) uploads a local image to GAR via docker push — no Cloud Build for the image itself, though [About](#About) and [Vouch](#Vouch) metadata still run in Cloud Build. 314:[Kludging](#Kludge) produces a local Docker image for development and testing without involving Cloud Build or the [Depot](#Depot). 318:The [Director](#Director) controls what enters the [Pouch](#Pouch) — Dockerfile, context files, build scripts — and the cloud receives only what the [Pouch](#Pouch) contains. 341:Two views are available: full ([SBOM](#SBOM), build info, Dockerfile) and compact (attestation summary). 452:If a compromised dependency, build plugin, or Dockerfile instruction executes during your build, an [Airgapped](#Airgap) build cannot phone home — it cannot transmit source code, secrets, or intermediate artifacts to an external endpoint, and it cannot silently fetch malicious payloads. 457:The attack surface collapses from "any URL a Dockerfile mentions" to "the specific digests the [Director](#Director) [Enshrined](#Enshrine)." 467:No framework mandates build-time network blocking by name, but egress-locked builds are the simplest way to evidence several common controls: FedRAMP CM-7 (least functionality) and SC-7 (boundary protection), SOC 2 CC6.1 (logical access) and CC8.1 (change management), and SLSA Level 3's hermetic build requirement. 483:[Recipe Bottle's](#RecipeBottle) Cloud Build architecture uses private pools, which are the prerequisite for VPC enforcement; enabling the controls themselves is deferred until organizational policy or external distribution requires them. 493:On macOS, both Docker and Podman run Linux containers inside a hidden Linux VM — there is no native container runtime on Darwin. 494:Podman support would require managing that VM's lifecycle within the customer's [Depot](#Depot), adding infrastructure complexity with no architectural advantage over Docker Desktop. 529:| ` │ ├── Dockerfile` | debian-slim + iptables + dnsmasq | 537:| ` │ ├── Dockerfile` | node:22-slim + SSH + Claude Code | 540:| ` │ ├── Dockerfile.tether` | Rust binary + network attack tools (tether build) | 541:| ` │ ├── Dockerfile.airgap` | Same image built `FROM` the forge ([Airgap](#Airgap) build, no upstream pull) | 545:| ` │ └── Dockerfile` | Forge image; consumed by `Dockerfile.airgap` as its base | 555:| ` │ ├── Dockerfile` | |

the compact view summarizes the attestation chain. 248:[Establishing](#Establish) walks the [Payor](#Payor) through project creation, API enablement, and consent screen configuration — the manual prerequisites before any automated operations can run. 291:**[Reliquary](#Reliquary)** — Co-versioned set of builder tool images (skopeo, docker, gcloud, syft) inscribed from upstream into the [Depot's](#Depot) registry. 310:[Grafting](#Graft) uploads a local image to GAR via docker push — no Cloud Build for the image itself, though [About](#About) and [Vouch](#Vouch) metadata still run in Cloud Build. 314:[Kludging](#Kludge) produces a local Docker image for development and testing without involving Cloud Build or the [Depot](#Depot). 318:The [Director](#Director) controls what enters the [Pouch](#Pouch) — Dockerfile, context files, build scripts — and the cloud receives only what the [Pouch](#Pouch) contains. 341:Two views are available: full ([SBOM](#SBOM), build info, Dockerfile) and compact (attestation summary). 452:If a compromised dependency, build plugin, or Dockerfile instruction executes during your build, an [Airgapped](#Airgap) build cannot phone home — it cannot transmit source code, secrets, or intermediate artifacts to an external endpoint, and it cannot silently fetch malicious payloads. 457:The attack surface collapses from "any URL a Dockerfile mentions" to "the specific digests the [Director](#Director) [Enshrined](#Enshrine)." 467:No framework mandates build-time network blocking by name, but egress-locked builds are the simplest way to evidence several common controls: FedRAMP CM-7 (least functionality) and SC-7 (boundary protection), SOC 2 CC6.1 (logical access) and CC8.1 (change management), and SLSA Level 3's hermetic build requirement. 483:[Recipe Bottle's](#RecipeBottle) Cloud Build architecture uses private pools, which are the prerequisite for VPC enforcement; enabling the controls themselves is deferred until organizational policy or external distribution requires them. 493:On macOS, both Docker and Podman run Linux containers inside a hidden Linux VM — there is no native container runtime on Darwin. 494:Podman support would require managing that VM's lifecycle within the customer's [Depot](#Depot), adding infrastructure complexity with no architectural advantage over Docker Desktop. 529:| ` │ ├── Dockerfile` | debian-slim + iptables + dnsmasq | 537:| ` │ ├── Dockerfile` | node:22-slim + SSH + Claude Code | 540:| ` │ ├── Dockerfile.tether` | Rust binary + network attack tools (tether build) | 541:| ` │ ├── Dockerfile.airgap` | Same image built `FROM` the forge ([Airgap](#Airgap) build, no upstream pull) | 545:| ` │ └── Dockerfile` | Forge image; consumed by `Dockerfile.airgap` as its base | 555:| ` │ ├── Dockerfile` | |

480	Naturally paired with conduit work: credentials and network access are configured together.
481	
482	- **VPC Service Controls** - Google Cloud security perimeters that prevent data from being copied out of a project even if an attacker holds valid credentials.
483	[Recipe Bottle's](#RecipeBottle) Cloud Build architecture uses private pools, which are the prerequisite for VPC enforcement; enabling the controls themselves is deferred until organizational policy or external distribution requires them.
484	If a VPC is stood up for the crucible conduit architecture, the VPC-SC perimeter should serve both Cloud Build [egress lockdown](#BuildIsolation) and [Bottle](#Bottle) conduit consumers.
485	
486	- **Cosign container signing** - Cryptographic image signatures independent of registry trust.
487	Deferred alongside VPC Service Controls until external distribution triggers the need.
488	
489	- **CDN-aware IP gating** - When allowed domains are CDN-hosted (e.g. Cloudflare), the [Sentry's](#Sentry) CIDR allowlist becomes coarse: DNS-level gating remains precise, but IP-level gating is porous across shared CDN address ranges.
490	A tighter mechanism is recognized but not yet designed.
491	
492	- **Podman support** - The spec accommodates Podman as an alternative container runtime, but support is deferred.
493	On macOS, both Docker and Podman run Linux containers inside a hidden Linux VM — there is no native container runtime on Darwin.
494	Podman support would require managing that VM's lifecycle within the customer's [Depot](#Depot), adding infrastructure complexity with no architectural advantage over Docker Desktop.
495	
496	- **[Crucible](#Crucible)-to-[Crucible](#Crucible) networking** - Under the current [Sentry](#Sentry) model, [Bottles](#Bottle) have no direct network path to each other; any inter-[Bottle](#Bottle) communication would route through their respective [Sentries](#Sentry).
497	The plumbing is feasible but not implemented, pending a concrete use case.
498	
499	## Appendix: Reference Project
500	
501	This repository is the reference implementation of [Recipe Bottle](#RecipeBottle).
502	The annotated tree below maps its files to the concepts defined above.
503	
504	| Path | Description |
505	|------|-------------|
506	| `Project Root/` | |
507	| `├── CLAUDE.md` | [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code) command reference, glossary, conventions |
508	| `├── RELEASE.md` | [Release Procedure](#Release-Procedure) — maintainer release qualification ceremony |
509	| `├── tt/` | 136 [Tabtargets](#Tabtarget) — `tt/rbw-<TAB>` for all operations |
510	| `├── Tools/` | |
511	| `│   ├── buk/` | Bash Utility Kit — portable CLI infrastructure |
512	| `│   └── rbk/` | Recipe Bottle Kit — domain logic |
513	| `├── .buk/` | [BURC](#BURC) project structure [Regime](#Regime) |
514	| `├── .rbk/` | [Regime](#Regime) configuration root |

file.content:
Naturally paired with conduit work: credentials and network access are configured together.

- **VPC Service Controls** - Google Cloud security perimeters that prevent data from being copied out of a project even if an attacker holds valid credentials.
[Recipe Bottle's](#RecipeBottle) Cloud Build architecture uses private pools, which are the prerequisite for VPC enforcement; enabling the controls themselves is deferred until organizational policy or external distribution requires them.
If a VPC is stood up for the crucible conduit architecture, the VPC-SC perimeter should serve both Cloud Build [egress lockdown](#BuildIsolation) and [Bottle](#Bottle) conduit consumers.

- **Cosign container signing** - Cryptographic image signatures independent of registry trust.
Deferred alongside VPC Service Controls until external distribution triggers the need.

- **CDN-aware IP gating** - When allowed domains are CDN-hosted (e.g. Cloudflare), the [Sentry's](#Sentry) CIDR allowlist becomes coarse: DNS-level gating remains precise, but IP-level gating is porous across shared CDN address ranges.
A tighter mechanism is recognized but not yet designed.

- **Podman support** - The spec accommodates Podman as an alternative container runtime, but support is deferred.
On macOS, both Docker and Podman run Linux containers inside a hidden Linux VM — there is no native container runtime on Darwin.
Podman support would require managing that VM's lifecycle within the customer's [Depot](#Depot), adding infrastructure complexity with no architectural advantage over Docker Desktop.

- **[Crucible](#Crucible)-to-[Crucible](#Crucible) networking** - Under the current [Sentry](#Sentry) model, [Bottles](#Bottle) have no direct network path to each other; any inter-[Bottle](#Bottle) communication would route through their respective [Sentries](#Sentry).
The plumbing is feasible but not implemented, pending a concrete use case.

## Appendix: Reference Project

This repository is the reference implementation of [Recipe Bottle](#RecipeBottle).
The annotated tree below maps its files to the concepts defined above.

| Path | Description |
|------|-------------|
| `Project Root/` | |
| `├── CLAUDE.md` | [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code) command reference, glossary, conventions |
| `├── RELEASE.md` | [Release Procedure](#Release-Procedure) — maintainer release qualification ceremony |
| `├── tt/` | 136 [Tabtargets](#Tabtarget) — `tt/rbw-<TAB>` for all operations |
| `├── Tools/` | |
| `│   ├── buk/` | Bash Utility Kit — portable CLI infrastructure |
| `│   └── rbk/` | Recipe Bottle Kit — domain logic |
| `├── .buk/` | [BURC](#BURC) project structure [Regime](#Regime) |
| `├── .rbk/` | [Regime](#Regime) configuration root |

content.text:
## Character
Human-driven with agent amending.

## Docket

Garrison-WSL exercise on the Windows host (post-AAt fenestrate-windows
admin trust, post-AAD WSL install).

**Pre-flight check addition (code amend)**: extend step 1 (or insert
step 0) in `bujb_jurisdiction.sh` Garrison flow so that for the `w`
shell-letter, an admin-SSH `wsl.exe --list --verbose` query runs
before the workload ceremony attempts `wsl.exe --distribution
${BUJB_wsl_distribution} --user root bash -s`. On mismatch (target
distribution absent or not the default), surface a clear BUK-side
diagnostic naming `BUJB_wsl_distribution` and pointing at AAD's
install procedure. Closes the paddock standing-note gap where the
current implementation fails opaquely through Microsoft's `wsl.exe`.

Curia-side: run `buw-jpGw <investiture>`. Step 6 knock proves
byte-exact privkey transport end-to-end through `bujb_jurisdiction.sh`
step 5 — incidentally validates the BBAA_ base64→openssl substitution
that the AAm-era docket marked as covered by live ceremony.

Agent amends `bujb_jurisdiction.sh`, handbook prose (`buw-hj0`), and
error diagnostics for issues surfaced.

Goal: WSL workload account garrisoned and reachable via the
workload-side ssh path; pre-flight WSL distribution check present and
surfaces a clear error when default distribution differs from
`BUJB_wsl_distribution`.

Operator prerequisites per BUS0 standing notes (workload pubkey
pre-registered with GitHub out-of-band, `BURP_WORKLOAD_KEY_FILE` in
place 0600 unencrypted).

Cygwin garrison (`buw-jpGc`) deferred to AAs.

host, sequenced after AAr's WSL path lands.

OpenSSH only).

end-to-end through Cygwin sshd.

diagnostics for Cygwin-specific friction.

host; b/c/w shell-letter support proven for c (in addition to AAr's
w).

content.text:
## Character
Investigation. Reproduction is known and reliable; root cause not classified.

## Docket
JJK localhost fundus scenario tests fail 6/8 in parallel and pass 8/8 sequentially.

Reproduce on curia at this commit or later (fundus at same commit):

- Fails 6/8: `cargo test --manifest-path Tools/jjk/vov_veiled/Cargo.toml --test jjtlg_fundus_scenario localhost::`
- Passes 8/8: append `-- --test-threads=1`

Failure pins in `zjjtlg_preflight_happy` (`Tools/jjk/vov_veiled/tests/jjtlg_fundus_scenario.rs`). The preflight SSH's `jjfu_full@localhost` and runs BUK tabtarget `buw-rcr.RenderConfigRegime.sh`; under concurrent SSH load this returns exit 1, while the same command run manually one-at-a-time returns exit 0. The contended path is BUK dispatch (writable state in logs / last-pointer / temp), not JJK — `buw-rcr` is BUK-side and unrelated to the BURH→BURN rename in AAW.

Pre-existing vs regression is not classified. First mount step: A/B against parent commit `44014598` (the BUF_EXT_ALIAS fix, before AAW). Investigation. Reproduction is known and reliable; root cause not classified.

JJK localhost fundus scenario tests fail 6/8 in parallel and pass 8/8 sequentially.

content.text:
## Character

Cleanup. Mechanical BCG conformance fixes on `Tools/buk/bujb_cli.sh`.

## Docket

Three classes of BCG drift in `bujb_cli.sh`, surfaced during review of
the L3 commit that landed `bujb_command_file`. Scope is this single
file; do not sweep siblings.

1. **Output filenames as kindle constants.** `stdout.log`, `stderr.log`,
   `exitcode` are written as bare literals against `BURD_OUTPUT_DIR`.
   BCG Template 3 prescribes kindle constants for fixed paths under
   `BURD_OUTPUT_DIR` (see the `Z«PREFIX»_OUTPUT_RESULT` example).
   Promote the three to `ZBUJB_OUTPUT_*` in `zbujb_kindle()` and
   update all call sites (the two ssh redirections, the exitcode
   write, and the `buc_die` message that references the stderr path).

2. **Unguarded write.** The `echo "${z_exit}" > .../exitcode` line
   has no `|| buc_die` per the no-silent-failures discipline that
   neighbouring code in this same file follows.

3. **Public command function entry shape.** The four public functions
   in this CLI (`bujb_resolve`, `bujb_knock`, `bujb_command_file`,
   `bujb_interactive_session`) skip the `zbujb_sentinel` first-line
   that BCG's boilerplate table mandates. Add the call. Do not audit
   sibling CLIs.

Also: `local -r` pass on assigned-once locals in this file
(`z_command_file` is the obvious one).

Done when:
- `bash -n Tools/buk/bujb_cli.sh` green
- `tt/buw-st.BukSelfTest.sh` green

1 # Recipe Bottle 2 3 [Recipe Bottle](#RecipeBottle) provides two independent container image capabilities: 4 5 - **[Foundry](#Foundry)**: orchestrate Google Cloud Build to produce multiplatform container images (x86 + ARM), fetch, retain, and serve them using a role-managed private cloud registry — with optional [egress-locked](#BuildIsolation) builds and supply-chain [provenance](#Provenance) 6 - **[Crucible](#Crucible)**: run untrusted containers behind enforced network isolation — DNS filtering and IP filtering — even using images unmodified from "in the wild" 7 8 > [!IMPORTANT] 9 > **Early-stage project — security review welcome in both domains** 10 > 11 > The [Foundry's](#Foundry) egress-locked Cloud Build configuration — including the [SLSA](#Provenance) attestation chain, [build isolation](#BuildIsolation), and digest-pinned toolchains — has not yet had broad independent review. 12 > 13 > The [Crucible](#Crucible) runtime containment — a multi-container apparatus where the workload runs unprivileged in a network namespace it does not control — has also not had broad review, particularly the network isolation rules, privileged namespace setup, and egress enforcement. 14 > 15 > If you evaluate or deploy this, you are contributing to its hardening. 16 > Security-focused contributors and responsible disclosure are especially valued. 17 18 [Recipe Bottle](#RecipeBottle) is a set of bash scripts enabling enterprise grade container image management intended for incorporation into any project. 19 The dependency footprint is deliberately narrow — `bash 3.2` and a handful of standard tools — with no Python runtime, no language-specific package manager, and no `gcloud` CLI. 20 After initial manual setup, all cloud API calls use `openssl` + `curl`. 21 A small team can stand up a hardened build pipeline and a sandboxed runtime without specialized DevOps expertise. 22 [Recipe Bottle's](#RecipeBottle) goal is a workflow where every container image has a verified origin and a controlled version, running behind appropriate network safeguards. 23 24 **Project page**: https://scaleinv.github.io/recipebottle 25 26

27 28

29 30 ## Environment 31 32 [Recipe Bottle](#RecipeBottle) is organized around two independent capabilities: the [Foundry](#Foundry) builds container images with verifiable [provenance](#Provenance), and the [Crucible](#Crucible) runs untrusted images with enforced network isolation. 33 The two compose but neither requires the other. 34 35 All configuration flows through [Regimes](#Regime) — structured `.env` files with typed validation, each with its own render and validate commands. 36 Some regimes are committed in the repo: [Vessel](#Vessel) definitions ([RBRV](#RBRV)), [Nameplate](#Nameplate) configurations ([RBRN](#RBRN)), [Depot](#Depot) identity ([RBRR](#RBRR)), and [Payor](#Payor) identity ([RBRP](#RBRP)). 37 Others live on the filesystem outside revision control: OAuth credentials ([RBRO](#RBRO)), role credentials ([RBRA](#RBRA)), and developer workstation paths ([BURS](#BURS)). 38 39 Every operation is launched through a [Tabtarget](#Tabtarget) — a shell script in the `tt/` directory. 40 The critical property: tab completion finds the command you want. 41 Type `tt/rbw-` and the shell narrows to all [Recipe Bottle](#RecipeBottle) operations; type `tt/rbw-h` to see just the [Hallmark](#Hallmark) commands. 42 Each [Tabtarget](#Tabtarget) is named `{colophon}.{frontispiece}.sh` — the colophon routes to the right module, the frontispiece tells you what it does. 43 44 Every state-changing [Tabtarget](#Tabtarget) writes three [Log](#Log) files to the directory named by `BURS_LOG_DIR` in your [BURS](#BURS) station file: a stable-name file (always the same path — easy for tooling to locate and evaluate the most recent run), a per-command file (same command name across runs — tools like SlickEdit sense diffs between executions), and a timestamped historical file (permanent record). 45 Disk space is cheap; [Log](#Log) unconditionally so the diagnostic evidence is always there when something fails. 46 Handbooks don't [Log](#Log) — teaching output is ephemeral. 47 48 A [Transcript](#Transcript) is a single file capturing key decision points and state transitions within a [Tabtarget's](#Tabtarget) execution. 49 Where [Logs](#Log) preserve full terminal output, a [Transcript](#Transcript) records the structured progress of sophisticated orchestration commands — the first thing to read when debugging a multi-step failure. 50 51 The [Output](#Output) directory is a fixed-path staging area cleared and recreated before each [Tabtarget](#Tabtarget) runs. 52 Commands that produce artifacts write them here. 53 Concurrent bash sessions share this path, so parallel commands can overwrite each other's [Output](#Output). 54 55 To begin, run the onboarding walkthrough: 56 57 ``` 58 tt/rbw-o.ONBOARDING.sh 59 ``` 60 61 ## Foundry 62 63 [Recipe Bottle's](#RecipeBottle) remote build orchestration system for producing, attesting, and distributing container images via Google Cloud Build and Google Artifact Registry. 64 The [Foundry](#Foundry) manages [Depot](#Depot) access, [Vessels](#Vessel) choreography, [Hallmark](#Hallmark) tracking, and build definitions. 65 Three [Vessel](#Vessel) modes determine how images enter the [Depot](#Depot): [Conjure](#Conjure) ([egress-locked](#BuildIsolation) build from source with [SLSA provenance](#Provenance)), [Bind](#Bind) (digest-pinned upstream mirror), and [Graft](#Graft) (local push). 66 Peer to [Crucible](#Crucible), which handles local runtime containment. 67 68 The [Foundry](#Foundry) orchestrates Google Cloud Build to produce container images with [SLSA](#Provenance) attestation, software bills of material, reproducible multi-architecture builds, and digest-pinned toolchains — so every image has a verifiable origin story. 69 Builds run in an [egress-locked](#BuildIsolation) configuration, drawing from upstream base images mirrored into a project-owned [Depot](#Depot) registry — a fixed, self-contained supply chain independent of third-party registry availability. 70 71 ### Depot 72 73 The facility where container images are built and stored — has its own GCP project with an artifact registry and a storage bucket, funded under the [Manor's](#Manor) billing account. 74 The [Payor](#Payor) [Levies](#Levy) a [Depot](#Depot), and the [Governor](#Governor) administers access to it. 75 Each [Depot](#Depot) operates as an independent supply-chain boundary with its own credentials, builds, and registry. 76 77 Each [Depot](#Depot) supports two build egress profiles: 78 79 - **[Tethered](#Tethered)** — Build egress mode allowing public internet access during Cloud Build. [Tethered](#Tethered) builds pull base images from upstream registries at build time — simpler to set up, but dependent on upstream availability. 80 - **[Airgap](#Airgap)** — Build egress mode with no public internet access during Cloud Build. 81 [Airgap](#Airgap) builds draw all dependencies from [Enshrined](#Enshrine) images in the [Depot's](#Depot) registry — fully self-contained, independent of upstream availability. 82 Requires [Enshrining](#Enshrine) base images before the first build. 83 See [Build Isolation](#BuildIsolation) for the security rationale behind these profiles. 84 85 ### Manor 86 87 The [Payor's](#Payor) administrative seat — holds the billing account, OAuth client, and operator identity. 88 [Depot](#Depot) projects are created and funded under the [Manor's](#Manor) authority. 89 The [Manor](#Manor) has its own GCP project, distinct from any [Depot](#Depot) project. 90
